JavaScript Core Concepts (6): Hoisting — Why You Can Use a Variable Before You Declare It
Hoisting is honestly one of my favorite JavaScript concepts. Every variable and every function in JavaScript has it built in.
The reason I like this concept so much is that you can use a variable before it's even been declared. Take this snippet:
the variable is declared. Forget for a second that JavaScript doesn't throw any error here — what's wild is that it spits out this magical value:

This behavior is what we call hoisting (沿用、拉升、Hoisting, take your pick). And here's a fun twist — if you go look up Hoisting on MDN, you might be a little surprised. The committee that defines JavaScript, ECMAScript (Ecma International), never actually coined this term. Hoisting is just a concept that got invented to help explain how JavaScript actually works under the hood.

JavaScript Hoisting refers to the process whereby the interpreter appears to move the declaration of functions, variables, classes, or imports to the top of their scope, prior to execution of the code.
One of the advantages of JavaScript putting function declarations into memory before it executes any code segment is that it allows you to use a function before you declare it in your code.
So you can split how JavaScript runs into two phases: the
. If we break down the snippet above, it looks like this:

Using the hoisting mental model to understand how JavaScript works is a solid approach. But I'll stress this one more time: "the whole hoisting process is really about memory, and JavaScript doesn't actually have this term in its spec."
When a variable or function gets declared, JavaScript carves out a spot for it in memory ahead of time. The big upside of this design is that your code can call or use a declaration
the line where it's declared. That's exactly why
instead of crashing the whole thing.
So why does the variable come back as
? Because when JavaScript reserves that memory ahead of time, it has to assign some initial value. If JavaScript didn't give the variable an initial value, you might actually get an error. So
is basically that placeholder initial value.
Functions get higher priority than variables

The example above was a variable. But functions, when declared, actually get higher priority than variables — a function gets created and placed into memory before a variable does. That said, this only applies to function statements. I'll cover the difference between function statements and function expressions in a later chapter. For now, just know that the function declarations we usually see are
get this early-into-memory treatment. Again, I'll explain function expressions in a later chapter.

functions get higher priority than variables
From this example you can see that the function gets created first because it has higher priority. If functions

Also, because functions get created first, you can call a function before the line where it's declared:
The course also had a little exercise that's great for understanding how this all works.

Creation phase:
Because functions have higher priority than variables, the function gets created first.
Note that at this point the function is built and ready to be called and run — but that doesn't mean
during the creation phase. The real execution only happens during the execution phase.
hasn't been assigned a value yet when

So in the end, the whole hoisting process really comes down to memory. And that's exactly why you can't find the term "Hoisting" anywhere in the ECMAScript spec — it's just a thing we made up to make understanding how JavaScript works a little easier.

Hoisting is honestly one of my favorite JavaScript concepts. Every variable and every function in JavaScript has it. In this post we'll dig into why you can use a variable before it's even declared, why you get undefined instead of an error, and why functions get higher priority than variables.
